frameworks.name: Bayesian belief updating
frameworks.one_line: Prior over choice alternatives updated trial-by-trial from internal evidence

frameworks.name: counterfactual confidence
frameworks.one_line: Confidence computed as if under a neutral prior to update base-rate belief

frameworks.name: perceptual decision making
frameworks.one_line: Random dot motion direction discrimination as the testbed task

core_question: How do humans learn prior probabilities of choice alternatives without feedback or teacher?
key_assumptions: Observers can compute confidence from internal evidence on each trial
Prior belief over alternatives can be updated using own decisions
A neutral-prior counterfactual confidence is representable alongside true confidence
manipulations: Base rate favoring left vs right varied across blocks of 15-42 trials
Withheld feedback and base-rate information from participants
key_findings: Participants gradually biased choices toward the prevailing base rate
Choice accuracy and confidence increased over a block without feedback
Behavior matched Bayesian updating of base-rate belief using counterfactual confidence
interpretations: Humans learn priors from their own decisions by simulating a neutral-prior confidence signal
Two confidence signals coexist: true confidence incorporating the prior and counterfactual confidence discounting it
